statusLine: European fuel security remains under pressure with 19 of 27 countries holding critical-level reserves in at least one fuel type, despite EU averages above minimum thresholds.
fullAnalysis:
The EU's overall fuel security position shows mixed signals in March 2026 data. While average reserves for petrol (96.3 days) and jet fuel (87.3 days) appear adequate, diesel stocks at 88.8 days are approaching the 90-day minimum threshold. However, these EU-wide averages mask significant vulnerabilities at the country level, where 19 of 27 member states are operating with critical reserves in at least one fuel category.

The most concerning situations exist in Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal, where all fuel types except jet fuel in Romania are at critical levels. Eastern European countries appear particularly vulnerable, with Poland showing severe shortages across all categories (petrol: 53.2 days, diesel: 65.1 days, jet fuel: 22.5 days). Meanwhile, Nordic and Baltic countries like Estonia, Latvia, and Finland demonstrate stronger reserve positions, though even Finland faces critical jet fuel stocks at 31.9 days.

Fuel pricing data from June reflects ongoing market pressures, with Brent crude at $86.95/barrel representing a significant 20% increase since late February escalations. This price environment, combined with the widespread reserve releases following Middle East supply disruptions, suggests European fuel markets remain under strain. The divergence between relatively stable EU averages and widespread country-level critical status indicates uneven distribution of reserves and potential supply chain bottlenecks that require continued monitoring.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries operate with critical fuel reserves in at least one category, despite EU averages appearing adequate
Diesel reserves at 88.8 days are approaching the mandatory 90-day minimum threshold EU-wide
Eastern European countries face the most severe shortages, with Poland showing critical levels across all fuel types
Jet fuel presents the broadest vulnerability, with 17 countries holding critical reserves despite an EU average of 87.3 days
Oil prices have surged 20% since February Middle East escalations, with Brent crude at $86.95/barrel adding cost pressure to already strained supplies
